data.title: Nokia, the company you once mocked, has seen its stock price rise by 70% this year.
data.description:
Author: Xiaobing, Deep Tide TechFlow

2In 2014, Microsoft spent $7.2 billion to buy Nokia's mobile phone business.

That year, everyone thought the story of this Finnish company, which had been around since 1865, was over.

The Nokia 3310, a phone that could crack walnuts, withstand being thrown against a wall without breaking, and even run Snake in the memories of a generation, along with the entire company behind it, has been relegated to the album of "tears of the times." Eleven years later, Jensen Huang called, offering them $1 billion. Nokia's stock has risen approximately 73% since the beginning of January this year, and 130% compared to the same period last year. This isn't a "dead cat bounce" in the stock market; it's an underestimated undercurrent in the entire AI narrative from 2025 to 2026. Moreover, almost no Chinese investors are seriously discussing it. Who is Justin Hotard? The story begins with a name. In February 2025, Nokia's board of directors announced that current CEO Pekka Lundmark would step down, and on April 1st, an American named Justin Hotard would take over. This is Nokia's first American-born CEO since its founding in 1865. Hotard is a typical example in the tech world of someone who is "not very famous but always riding the wave of success." He holds a bachelor's degree in Electrical Engineering from the University of Illinois and an MBA from MIT Sloan. He worked at Hewlett-Packard (HPE) for eight and a half years, eventually becoming the head of the High Performance Computing and AI Labs, where he personally delivered the world's first exascale supercomputer to the U.S. Department of Energy. Then, in early 2024, he was poached by Intel to head the Data Center and AI business unit, reporting directly to Pat Gelsinger. Note his resume. HPC, data centers, and AI—these three keywords have absolutely nothing to do with Nokia's image over the past decade. What did Nokia do? It made base stations, telecommunications equipment, fiber optic cables, and sold them to operators. A typical, slow-moving, and forgotten "old European" hardware company. But in early 2025, Nokia's board of directors made a seemingly illogical decision: they didn't want someone knowledgeable in telecommunications; they wanted someone knowledgeable in AI. In the appointment announcement, Chairman Sari Baldauf stated: "AI and the data center market are key areas for Nokia's future growth." At that moment, almost no one took it seriously. The market reaction was muted, and the stock price rose slightly. All analysts were writing predictable analyses like, "The Finnish powerhouse changes leadership; can the newcomer turn the tide?" No one realized that the company was quietly changing its engine.

An Undervalued Acquisition

If you only look at Hotard's appointment, it's just a routine executive rotation. But if you compare it with another event from six months ago, the script changes completely.

In June 2024, Nokia announced a $2.3 billion acquisition of an American company—Infinera.

What kind of company is Infinera? It does optical networking, which, simply put, is the "fiber optic communication equipment" between data centers and between racks.

If you've ever talked to someone working on AI infrastructure, you'll know this fact: The biggest bottleneck in AI data centers isn't GPUs, it's optical communication. Nvidia can cram 72 GPUs into a single rack, and these GPUs need to exchange data frantically. A data center might have tens of thousands of GPUs, and they also need to exchange data. Two data centers also need to synchronize training data. With each additional cluster, the demand for optical modules grows exponentially. This is why, in the past two years, the stock prices of optical module companies like Coherent in the US, and InnoLight and Eoptolink in China, have skyrocketed. Infinera is one of the few companies that simultaneously possesses two core technologies: photonic integrated circuits (PIC) and data center interconnects. It already has established customer relationships with North American hyperscale cloud providers (Microsoft, Amazon, Google). When Nokia signed this deal in June 2024, the market interpreted it as: "A traditional telecommunications company acquired a traditional fiber optic company—a 'typical' story of two elephants huddling together for warmth." However, after the transaction was completed in February 2025 and Infinera was consolidated into Nokia's financial statements, the financial figures of this long-established Finnish company began to change: In 2025, revenue from optical networking services increased by 17% year-on-year. In the first quarter of 2026, optical networking sales reached €821 million, a year-on-year increase of 20%, surpassing IP and core software to become Nokia's second-largest business. Sales contributed by AI and cloud customers surged by 49% year-on-year in a single quarter. The most crucial figure is this: In the first quarter of 2026, AI and cloud customers placed orders with Nokia totaling €1 billion. What does this figure mean? It's equivalent to a quarter's worth of orders, exceeding Infinera's entire sales revenue for the year before its acquisition. And all of this went almost unnoticed outside of Western tech media. What truly sent shockwaves through the market was October 28, 2025. On that day, at the GTC conference in Washington, Nvidia announced: Nvidia would invest $1 billion in Nokia at a price of $6.01 per share. Note this detail: "$6.01 per share" is a subscription price, not a market price. Nvidia didn't buy shares on the secondary market; Nokia specifically issued a batch of new shares to Nvidia. Nvidia's investment was strategic, not just financial. Why did Jensen Huang invest $1 billion in Nokia? Nvidia's official statement is that the two companies will jointly develop AI-RAN (AI Radio Access Network). Nokia's 5G and 6G software will be ported to Nvidia's CUDA platform; Nvidia's Arc-Pro accelerator, specifically developed for the telecom industry, will be embedded in Nokia's base stations. T-Mobile US became the first pilot operator. Dell provided the servers. This sounds like another common "AI empowering XX industry" story. But the real surprise lies in a technical detail that 99% of people won't notice. First, you need to know some background: Nokia is not the only player in the AI-RAN race. Its biggest competitor is Ericsson, also from Northern Europe. Ericsson and Nokia seem to do exactly the same thing: providing 5G/6G base station equipment to operators. But in the matter of "how to fit a GPU into a base station," they've taken two completely opposite paths. Engineers jokingly call these two paths a "religious war." The first path is called "Lookaside" (bypass acceleration). This is the path Ericsson and Intel are taking. Simply put: the CPU in the base station is still the main controller, while the GPU is just a "helper." When an acceleration task needs to be performed, the CPU "passes" the task to the GPU, which then "returns" it after processing. Data jumps back and forth between the CPU and GPU repeatedly. The second approach is called Inline (online acceleration). This is the path Nokia and Nvidia are taking. Simply put: network data received by the base station first enters the GPU, which processes it before handing it over to the CPU. The GPU becomes the main player, and the CPU becomes a supporting role. Sounds like just a matter of engineering sequence? No, this is a fundamental disagreement about "who will be the computing center in the future." The very existence of Nvidia is to prove that GPUs should be the center of data processing, and CPUs should give way. The entire CUDA ecosystem is designed with a "GPU-centric" philosophy. The Lookaside approach, from its architecture, presupposes that "CPUs will still be number one," which is fundamentally incompatible with Nvidia's worldview. Therefore, when Nvidia needs a telecom partner, it cannot choose Ericsson. It must choose a partner willing to place GPUs at the center. Nokia is that partner. This is why this $1 billion isn't just a simple "strategic investment." Jensen Huang has personally stamped his mark on a new chapter in the AI ​​narrative; he's buying an entry point that will allow Nvidia's GPUs to reach 5 million base stations worldwide. According to Omdia's forecast, the cumulative market size of AI-RAN will exceed $200 billion by 2030. If this story is told correctly, this $1 billion investment by Jensen Huang could be one of the most profitable investments of his lifetime. Geopolitics played a role. Nokia's comeback also had a sensitive undercurrent. On April 13, 2026, Bank of America analyst Oliver Wong upgraded Nokia from "neutral" to "buy," significantly raising his target price from €6.87 to €10.70. Nokia's stock price surged 9.67% that day, with trading volume increasing by 178% compared to the 3-month average. In that report, Oliver Wong listed four reasons why Nokia was undervalued. The third reason, though worded subtly, is very clear: "After European countries gradually restricted Huawei and ZTE, Nokia has effectively become 'the last Western sovereign supplier to choose from.'" In plain terms: Europe wants to build sovereign data centers and sovereign 5G/6G networks. Chinese equipment is unusable, and there are no such companies in the US. The only remaining Western suppliers are Nokia and Ericsson. Ericsson lacks full-stack capabilities in optical networks, Infinera was acquired by Nokia, and Cisco is an American company. Therefore, the money for European sovereign cloud projects almost exclusively flows to Nokia. This is a classic example of a "geopolitical arbitrage" opportunity. The changing international order presented Nokia with a significant gift; as long as Nokia remained in the game, it could reap the benefits. Add to this the demand for optical networks from US hyperscale cloud providers and T-Mobile's bet on AI-RAN, and three streams of capital simultaneously poured into Nokia from three directions. It took the market 18 months to react. Piecing together all the clues, you'll find a very dramatic timeline: June 2024: Nokia announced the acquisition of Infinera. February 2025: Hotard was appointed as the new CEO. October 2025: Nvidia invested $1 billion. 2026... On April 13, 2026, Bank of America upgraded its rating, and the stock price rose by 9.67% in a single day. On April 22, 2026, the Q1 financial report disclosed €1 billion in AI/cloud orders, and optical network business increased by 20%. On April 27, 2026, CFRA doubled its target price from $8 to $16, and Nokia's stock price hit a new high since 2015. Did you notice? The fundamentals began to change as early as 18 months ago. But it took the market 18 months to connect these clues. This is the classic process of "value discovery." When a story hasn't been fully explained, everyone treats it as "old wine in an old bottle"; by the time the story is clear, the valuation has already recovered significantly. Nokia's current P/E ratio is 26 times forward PE, which isn't expensive for a 17% growing optical networking business. But compared to its lows at the beginning of the year, it's no longer the "forgotten stock" lying on the ground. Over the past two years, Chinese investors have been focused on Nvidia, TSMC, Broadcom, and AMD—the engines of this AI wave. But beyond the engine, there are also gearboxes, drive shafts, tires, and highways. The AI ​​narrative is spreading from "chips" to "pipes." The story of optical module manufacturers has been told for over a year; the next thing to be repriced by the market may be base stations, fiber optic cables, data center power, and cooling systems. The story won't repeat itself, but it will rhyme. When a new technological paradigm truly arrives, the biggest alpha may not be in the obvious places. It's in those corners you thought were "forgotten."

data.title: Musk sues Altman, seeking $134 billion in damages.
data.description:
The hottest news in Silicon Valley's tech world recently is the lawsuit filed by Elon Musk, the world's richest man, against OpenAI CEO Sam Altman. The lawsuit seeks a staggering $134 billion! OpenAI, touted as the most successful company in history in terms of fundraising, has only secured a total of $122 billion in confirmed funding. Therefore, Musk is essentially trying to destroy OpenAI, even taking time out of his busy schedule to personally appear in court. In 2015, OpenAI was co-founded by Elon Musk, Dennis Altman, and others, positioning itself as a non-profit AI lab "aimed at benefiting all of humanity." Musk contributed money, personnel, and fame; he stated that he invested at least $44 million in the early stages of its founding. However, in 2019, OpenAI was restructured into a "profit-capped" entity, with Microsoft investing $1 billion. In 2025, OpenAI completed its capital restructuring, officially transforming into a "Type B company," with Microsoft holding approximately 27% and the OpenAI Foundation holding 26%. Musk said: This is betrayal, calling Sam Altman "Scam Altman," and stating that the current OpenAI is no longer the non-profit organization founded to "benefit all mankind," but has become a "cash cow" for Microsoft. OpenAI's rebuttal was equally direct: they countered with a pile of old emails as evidence, pointing out that Musk's departure from OpenAI wasn't due to so-called "disillusionment," but rather because of a failed power grab—he had wanted to merge OpenAI into Tesla, but everyone disagreed. Both sides presented their own arguments, leading to this lawsuit. Normally, U.S. courts select juries from among residents aged 18 and over in their jurisdiction who do not know the plaintiff or defendant. However, the Oakland Federal Court in California could not find any adult residents who did not know Musk. Therefore, the court had to relax the standard, no longer requiring jurors to be unaware of Musk, but only requiring them to "set aside preconceived notions and make judgments solely based on the evidence presented in court."

So, after spending more than 5 hours, 9 jurors were selected from a pool of candidates, including nurses, insurance salesmen, and paint sellers, among others.

The judge believed that the core of this case was "whether the charitable trust was breached," which did not require deep learning expertise, but only an understanding of "whether promises made should be kept."

However, anyone who pays even a little attention to the financial markets knows that Musk's SpaceX is currently rushing to IPO, and OpenAI is also expected to IPO by the end of this year.

Both projects have market capitalizations exceeding one trillion dollars, and OpenAI will definitely take a portion of SpaceX's funds.

If Musk wins this lawsuit, the court may force OpenAI to revert to a non-profit structure, effectively jeopardizing its IPO. If the court does award Musk the $134 billion in damages, OpenAI will immediately face a cash flow crisis.

data.title: ZachXBT Flags Worldcoin Concerns Over Low-Income User Targeting And Growing Black Market Identity Claims
data.description:
The dispute surrounding Sam Altman’s Worldcoin project has escalated again, with fresh allegations over biometric data practices and token distribution drawing renewed attention from crypto analysts, regulators, and industry figures.

At the centre of the criticism are concerns that the project’s iris-scanning identity system and its native token, Worldcoin [WLD], may be structured in ways that disproportionately affect vulnerable users while concentrating value among insiders.

Altman Under Pressure As Worldcoin Model Faces Fresh Scrutiny

Questions around Sam Altman have intensified after on-chain investigator ZachXBT publicly criticised Worldcoin, now branded “World,” describing its incentive model as exploitative and comparable to past high-profile crypto failures.

The backlash was amplified after Elon Musk referred to Altman as “Scam Altman” on X, prompting ZachXBT to respond directly, accusing the project of using small token rewards to attract users in low-income regions while collecting sensitive biometric data.

No one seems to mention Scam Altman’s other company WorldCoin aka World which launched with a predatory low float crypto token $WLD that was on par with SBF / FTX companies.

They have preyed on people from low income countries for biometric data by giving away small amounts of… pic.twitter.com/dZR0X1pJzt

— ZachXBT (@zachxbt) April 28, 2026

Worldcoin’s system relies on its “Orb” device, which scans users’ irises to create a unique digital identity intended to verify human users online.

Participants are rewarded with WLD tokens in return.

ZachXBT argued this structure has contributed to the emergence of informal trading markets for verified identities, stating,

“The actual black market for KYC’d accounts is always on Telegram if you know where to look.”

He further claimed that Worldcoin’s model has led to accounts being traded on underground channels for as little as $5–$15, raising concerns over the real-world use of biometric-linked identities.

World / WorldCoin verified accounts going for $5-$15 pic.twitter.com/FMx8EjeQKC

— ZachXBT (@zachxbt) August 18, 2025

Token Supply Concentration And Insider Sales Under Question

Beyond data concerns, scrutiny has also turned to WLD’s tokenomics, with critics pointing to what they describe as highly concentrated supply dynamics and ongoing insider activity.

Blockchain analytics from CoinMarketCap suggest that roughly 10 wallets control around 69% of the WLD supply, reinforcing concerns about centralisation.

ZachXBT described WLD as a “low float” asset, comparing its structure to controversial token models linked to the collapse of FTX, while alleging that insiders continue to sell tokens through over-the-counter channels.

Data cited from Arkham Intelligence indicates that Worldcoin-linked wallets have sold around $65 million worth of WLD over the past 90 days.

🚨 BREAKING: The World Foundation has confirmed the sale of $65 million in WLD tokens via subsidiary World Assets Ltd.

The transactions involve ~239 million WLD tokens sold to four private counterparties at an average price of $0.2719. Notably, WLD hit an all-time low of $0.2444… pic.twitter.com/uahVegh99Z

— Crypto Trend Daily (@cryptotrendaily) March 29, 2026

Meanwhile, token issuance continues at a steady pace.

According to DeFiLlama data, approximately $1.21 million worth of WLD is unlocked daily, a schedule expected to run until July 2029, when emissions are projected to decline to around $402,000 per day.

Black Market Claims And Regulatory Pushback Intensify Concerns

Critics have long argued that Worldcoin’s incentive system has encouraged participation without full understanding of how biometric data is used.

ZachXBT alleged that the project targeted individuals in lower-income regions with small token incentives in exchange for iris scans, contributing to what he described as a distorted market for verified identities.

He also referenced earlier claims that recruitment tactics involved unclear communication and incentives such as small consumer goods in some regions, further intensifying ethical concerns.

Regulatory responses have varied globally.

Kenya was among the first to suspend operations over data security concerns in 2023, followed by temporary restrictions in Spain.

Other jurisdictions, including Portugal, Germany, Brazil, Indonesia, Thailand, and Hong Kong, have also launched investigations or ordered the deletion of collected biometric data.

Token Collapse Deepens Market Skepticism

WLD’s market performance has added to the criticism.

The token reached a peak near $11.82 in March 2024 but has since fallen more than 97%, trading around $0.25–$0.26 by April 2026.

Analysts point to continuous token unlocks, high inflation rates, and reported secondary sales as key drivers behind sustained selling pressure.

ZachXBT previously described the token as “the biggest scam token of the bull run,” alleging misleading marketing and coordinated behaviour among early backers and insiders.

It’s not criticism when you are complicit in the biggest token scam of the bull run as a team member. https://t.co/gXHBY3vdo2pic.twitter.com/dhSxq48i1R

— ZachXBT (@zachxbt) July 17, 2024

Musk And Altman Rivalry Returns To The Spotlight

The renewed debate around Worldcoin comes as long-standing tensions between Altman and Musk resurface publicly.

Both co-founded OpenAI in 2015, but Musk left in 2018 after disagreements over direction and governance.

He has since accused Altman of shifting OpenAI away from its original nonprofit mission.

Musk’s ongoing lawsuit against OpenAI, which centres on allegations of mission deviation and commercial restructuring, is set to proceed to trial in 2026, with damages reportedly valued at $150 billion.

Recent prediction market data shows sentiment shifting notably around the case.

On Polymarket, the probability of Elon Musk’s victory has dropped sharply to around 35%, down from nearly 60% previously, suggesting a rapid reassessment of the case’s outlook among traders.

OpenAIをめぐるElon MuskとSam Altmanの裁判。

Polymarketではイーロンマスク勝利の確率が35%まで低下。

一時は60%近くあったが、直近1日で大きく下落した。

市場は現時点では、イーロン・マスク有利とは見ていない模様 pic.twitter.com/DWRho4WqmW

— 荒井健一 AK (@aarai666) April 29, 2026

Altman has previously rejected claims of wrongdoing, while Musk has continued to publicly criticise both OpenAI and Worldcoin, keeping tensions between the two figures firmly in the spotlight.

data.title: Bitbank Launches Japan’s First Bitcoin-Settled Credit Card, Blurring Line Between Credit and Crypto
data.description:
Japanese cryptocurrency exchange Bitbank has introduced a new crypto-linked credit card that allows users to settle monthly credit card bills directly in Bitcoin held on their exchange accounts, marking one of the most advanced integrations of crypto assets into regulated payment infrastructure in Japan to date.

Bitbank launches Japan’s first crypto-linked credit card

Bitbank says it has introduced Japan’s first crypto-linked credit card, allowing users to pay directly in Bitcoin from their exchange balance.

Bitbank’s crypto card is another step toward mainstream usage—turning Bitcoin… pic.twitter.com/rA4cr3HK49

— Time News (@TimeNewsIND) April 28, 2026

The move signals a deeper shift in how Japan’s licensed crypto ecosystem is approaching everyday financial services, with Bitcoin increasingly positioned not just as a store of value but at the forefront of the country's traditional financial system.

Credit Infrastructure Meets Bitcoin Settlement

Unlike conventional crypto debit or prepaid cards, Bitbank’s new product functions just like any other credit card, where users spend through standard merchant payment rails and later settle their monthly balance directly in Bitcoin rather than Japanese yen.

Branded as the “EPOS Crypto Card for Bitbank,” the product is issued in partnership with EPOS Card, the fintech arm of Marui Group, and is the first in Japan to enable credit card bill settlement directly from crypto exchange balances.

今日から、BTCでクレジットカードの支払いができるようになりました。

丸井グループ×bitbankが「EPOS CRYPTOカード for bitbank」を本日発行開始。銀行口座の代わりにbitbankのBTC残高から引き落とせる、日本初のサービスです。還元はBTC・ETH・ASTRの3銘柄から選択可能。

先週のSlash… https://t.co/qtflJfTmYJ

— Bit_news (@Bitnews2025) April 27, 2026

At launch, Bitcoin is the only supported settlement asset, with potential expansion to other cryptocurrencies such as Ether and Aster under consideration.

The card also introduces a 0.5% cryptocurrency cashback system on all spending, with rewards credited directly back into users’ Bitbank accounts. Depending on future updates, cashback may be distributed in Bitcoin, Ether, or Aster, reinforcing the exchange-based ecosystem loop between spending and asset accumulation.

The launch comes amid rising competition in Japan’s crypto payments sector. Earlier entrants such as Binance Japan introduced crypto-linked cards offering rewards in BNB, but Bitbank’s model differentiates itself by focusing on credit settlement directly from exchange-held assets rather than prepaid or debit-based spending structures.

Japan warns unregistered crypto in real‑estate deals could breach the law, citing high money‑laundering risk. Land Ministry, FSA, National Police and Finance Ministry call for tighter oversight — property tokenization and peer‑to‑peer transfers under scrutiny.…

— invezz.com (@InvezzPortal) April 28, 2026

This positions Bitbank’s offering as a more deeply integrated financial product rather than a spending bridge layered on top of fiat systems.

Regulated Timing and Structural Significance

Operating under Japan’s Financial Services Agency licensing framework since 2017, Bitbank is leveraging one of the most clearly defined regulatory environments in global crypto markets to push further into payments infrastructure.

Japan’s relatively mature regulatory stance has enabled exchanges to experiment with financial products that remain restricted in many other jurisdictions. Bitbank’s approach reflects a broader trend of licensed crypto platforms embedding digital assets directly into consumer finance rather than limiting them to trading or investment use cases.

🇯🇵 JAPAN’S CENTRAL BANK ANNOUNCED TO TEST CRYPTO-BASED PAYMENT SYSTEM FOR THEIR NATIONAL CURRENCY

HERE WE GO 🚀 pic.twitter.com/1aLSDjYVko

— Bitcoin Teddy (@Bitcoin_Teddy) April 23, 2026

With Japan maintaining one of the world’s highest credit card penetration rates, the introduction of Bitcoin-settled credit products could meaningfully expand crypto’s role in everyday transactions, particularly among existing BTC holders seeking frictionless spending mechanisms without exiting their positions.

data.title: Tether CEO: Building Open Source Infrastructure to Achieve Financial Inclusion
data.description:
Compiled by: Jinse Finance

Tether CEO Paolo Ardoino delivered a speech at the 2026 Bitcoin Conference in Las Vegas. In his speech, Paolo Ardoino introduced Tether's newly released The Resilience Stack, which integrates its peer-to-peer communication protocol HolePunch, decentralized communication application Keet, open-source self-hosted wallet toolkit WDK, and local AI development platform QVAC into an infrastructure system for people without access to basic financial and communication services. Keet was previously not open-source, but Ardoino formally pledged to promote its open-source development, and related documentation and modules are currently being prepared. Furthermore, Paolo Ardoino stated that Tether has launched the BTC Faucet; users who download the Tether wallet application can instantly receive a small amount of Bitcoin via the Lightning Network by replying to the Tether Wallet tweet as required. In his speech, Paolo Ardoino began with a core metaphor from Isaac Asimov's psychohistorical theory in his Foundation series, explaining that Tether's fundamental mission is not merely to issue the USDT stablecoin and accumulate Bitcoin in the long term, but also to address the "global dark crisis" of social unrest, systemic turmoil, inflation, financial exclusion, and power monopolies. He argued that Tether aims to enhance social resilience and bridge the development gap by building a long-term technological system. Ardoino pointed out that multiple real crises have already emerged globally: hundreds of millions of people face unstable electricity supply, large populations are excluded from the traditional financial system, and large corporations and institutions monopolize technology and unilaterally exploit users, leading to a slow and continuous destabilization of society. Simultaneously, nearly half the population lacks access to basic financial services, and the rapid development of AI technology will further amplify the global wealth gap and digital literacy disparity, exacerbating social divisions. Paolo Ardoino stated that the core of combating systemic decay is not short-term resistance, but rather building a long-term, decentralized, and censorship-resistant underlying infrastructure. This is also the core motivation behind Tether's creation of The Resilience Stack. Paolo Ardoino explained that around The Resilience Stack, Tether has launched a full-chain open-source technology ecosystem, constructing a decentralized system layer by layer: the bottom layer is the HolePunch peer-to-peer communication protocol, which is serverless, censorship-resistant, and highly scalable, reshaping decentralized network transmission capabilities; on top of this is the censorship-free instant messaging application Keet, enabling secure and private global communication, which will be fully open-sourced in the future; relying on the open-source wallet library WDK, it promotes self-custodial wallets, adapting to the peer-to-peer Bitcoin payment needs of ordinary people, smart devices, and AI Agnet, providing solutions for high-frequency micro-transactions in the era of the Internet of Things; the top layer implements the QVAC local decentralized AI tool, ensuring user data privacy, adapting to low-end devices and remote areas, and practicing the philosophy that "AI that doesn't belong to you isn't your intelligence." In addition, Paolo Ardoino stated that Tether boasts a massive global user base, covering 160 countries and over 573 million users, with its ecosystem experiencing rapid growth. It also continues to invest heavily in open-source development, having launched over a thousand open-source projects. Tether plans to attract global developers to co-build its ecosystem through funding, hackathons, and other means. In his speech, Paolo Ardoino emphasized that Tether's long-term vision is to break down corporate and institutional monopolies by leveraging an integrated open-source technology stack encompassing communications, finance, and decentralized AI. This will achieve financial inclusion, freedom of communication, and controllable privacy, relying on the public and decentralized technology to build a stable order, mitigate potential global risks, and complete long-term societal repair and innovation. The following is the full text of Paolo Ardoino's speech, compiled by Jinse Finance (with Deepseek assistance). I'm very happy to be here again this year. Why am I showing this video? I want to explain as best as I can how we think about Tether. Tether is well-known as a stablecoin company, issuing USDT, and is a buyer of Bitcoin. We hold over 130,000 to 140,000 Bitcoins. We're constantly buying. Just today, we launched a Bitcoin faucet. If you're on Twitter, have a Tether BTC account, and you can do this using your Tether.me email address (which you'll get when you download your Tether wallet), you'll receive 10 cents of Bitcoin directly into your wallet for free. Yes, we do all these things, but I want to explain to you why Tether is so much more than that. The best way I can think of to draw inspiration is from Isaac Asimov, my all-time favorite author. If you've read Asimov's Foundation series, there's a character, a mathematician named Hari Seldon, who created a theory called psychohistory.

Psychohistory is a theory, a combination of mathematics, statistics, physics, sociology, and political science; it is a complete science used to understand how humanity and society will transform, shape, and change over hundreds, thousands, or millions of years. This is an excerpt from the book. We use this to express—as Asimov very eloquently puts it in the book—that you can use science to predict and analyze what is happening in the universe, predict the outcome, and while predicting the outcome, try to think about how to use the same science to adjust it, push things in a different direction, change the course of social decay, and change the potential darkness that may befall us all. Just as in the book, Asimov describes this Hari Seldon prediction that after the Empire (the most powerful empire in the galaxy) has existed for ten thousand years, something will happen, a turmoil will occur, followed by ten thousand years of darkness. He also predicts that, by using psychohistory, the darkness can be shortened to only a few hundred years.

Why am I telling you this? Why did I bring up these apocalyptic discussions at the Bitcoin conference?

I think **my way of thinking about Bitcoin is that it's a technology, the beginning of a new social structure, creating a new spark. This spark will survive and resist any darkness because it's designed to be peer-to-peer. It's created by people, by the people who use it, by the people in every corner of the world who accept it.** So, if we believe this is indeed true, if we believe Bitcoin is indeed the first element in the potential struggle against the coming darkness, what can we learn from it? First, when I think about what Asimov was trying to convey through his psychohistorical science, I wonder, is this just science fiction? Does it only happen in books? Then I think that the darkness described in Asimov's books can actually be reduced to and brought back to Earth, because the darkness described in those books is nothing more than social chaos, social instability. You may have heard me mention last year that Tether, to us—we call it the "stability company" internally—is a company that aims to bring stability to society. Hari Seldon, in Asimov's books, attempted to solve and reduce the darkness from ten thousand years to a few hundred years through the science of psychohistory. And in Tether, we believe the darkness that is coming in the societies we are seeing—it's there. We see war, we see inflation, we see our currencies being destroyed, we see instability, we see all the signs that what we call the coming darkness—the world isn't necessarily heading for a better place; there's more chaos, more instability. So I wanted to use this video to explain how, starting with the universe and science fiction, we can actually reduce it to what's happening on Earth. Let me move on to the next part. This is a story that not only predicts the future but also identifies the present. Right now, 700 million people are living on intermittent power. They are already in the dark. Families that have farmed for generations to support their communities are abandoned overnight because the system deems them economically unviable. In one of the oldest democracies on Earth, people are arrested not for violence, but for their speech, for an emoji. The lessons of some governments are clear: they don't need to silence us; they just need to make sure we can't afford our rent. In the most powerful countries, corporations are building machines not to serve the people, but to exploit them. The darkness doesn't come with an explosion, but with a slow, gradual darkening. So, let's return to Earth from space science fiction. The decades of darkness in the universe described in the book translates to a more unstable and unpredictable society on Earth. Hundreds of millions—in fact, billions—lack access to basic financial services. They lack electricity and reliable telecommunications. More importantly, they lack, and will not have access to, basic smart services. Consider that four billion people worldwide lack access to basic financial services. They are left behind by the traditional financial system. How can we assume that excluding half the world's population will bring more stability to society? This is the analogy we're trying to draw from the revelations of psychohistory: if we do nothing about it, if we don't do something, if we don't try to use science, technology, and our abilities to build something different—something that lasts longer than darkness, something that reduces darkness by creating stability, by creating points of light in nations and cities, in people, something that connects people (wherever they are) and makes them resilient to change—that will be the darkness on our planet. But you know what all this talk means? What does Tether intend to do about it? Let's read on. Hari Seldon understands what most people miss: you don't fight darkness by winning a battle, you fight it by building a system that can outlast darkness. That's what we're doing. A foundation, real infrastructure, we call "The Resilience Stack." That's our answer. The Resilience Stack is an open-source technology stack, a scientific approach, like a case of psychohistory, analyzing the different problems we see in all the countries we operate in. Remember, Tether operates in hundreds of countries, in 160 different countries. We have people on the ground. We talk to people. We have a network of 573 million users using USDT, Tether Gold, and all the other services we offer, and that network is growing. Our network is growing by 34 million new wallets every quarter. That's unprecedented. This scale proves that the technology we're building is growing at the speed of a social network. It's no longer just a fintech company, no longer just a stablecoin; it's becoming a movement, a technology stack that's becoming part of the fabric of the world—a world where parents are left behind by the traditional financial system, left behind by the ability to communicate securely with their children, and who may not even have electricity in their homes. Imagine a world where half the world's population lacks access to basic financial services and doesn't participate in the 100-fold increase in intelligence capabilities brought about by artificial intelligence. If the gap between the two sides of society is already so large due to financial inclusion, imagine what will happen when AI truly becomes a part of everyone's life. This gap, which is currently dividing two large halves of the world's population, will widen a hundredfold because of AI. The Resilience Stack is our response to this problem. What are the concrete, tangible things? What are the actual technologies, like those in psychohistory, that we can use, build, and apply to reduce dark ages from centuries or decades to just a few years and create those sparks throughout society? In this way, no matter what happens, no matter how dystopian the future, no matter what disaster, no matter what pandemic, no matter what, we can still connect. We can transact together with Bitcoin. We can use AI services that serve us, not just a few large corporations. All of this comes together in a single story, a single stack. You should look at Tether's GitHub, the open-source code repository. I think we've crossed 1000 open-source projects on GitHub. That's quite unique. It shows that we really care deeply about building something that lasts longer than our company, because software technology needs to be resilient to its creators as well. That's also one of the things I love about Bitcoin: it lasts longer than its creators, it will last forever. Of course, we will always remember Satoshi Nakamoto. We are all Satoshi Nakamoto. But that's the beauty of technology. It means that when a technology is done well, everyone can use it, no matter who created it. Everyone is actually a father of that technology. Let's look at the very bottom, the first part. A peer-to-peer protocol, no servers, no central authority to maintain it. We've built **HolePunch**, a fully peer-to-peer telecommunications protocol that scales to billions of users, billions of machines, and trillions of AI agents. For the more tech-savvy among you, it's like the BitTorrent protocol built about 30 years ago, rewritten from scratch, improved, with added encryption layers, making it incredibly scalable and adaptable for not only file sharing but also any real-time data streaming for video calls, messaging, mapping, and much more. It's completely open source. Everyone should check it out because the HolePunch protocol is the first to allow any developer to build applications that scale to billions of people and hundreds of thousands of companies without any single central server. Building on that, we've proven—and we'll see later—that we ourselves have the capability to provide a truly scalable service to all of humanity without any single point of failure. You can build any application on top of it. Similarly, you can build a peer-to-peer Uber, a peer-to-peer mapping system, anything you want. It's free and one of the most sophisticated yet easy-to-use software and networking stacks, empowering in incredible ways anything you build for your company, yourself, your family, or your friends. In my view, this is like Bitcoin to finance. HolePunch to the internet and telecommunications is like Bitcoin to finance. On top of that, there is communication that cannot be suppressed or shut down.

On top of the HolePunch protocol and stack, we built Keet. I'm not sure how many of you have used Keet. It's an example of a messaging application with over 5 million users on desktop and mobile, probably more, because it's hard to count because there's no central server. There are millions of users and chat rooms with tens of thousands of users sharing tens of millions of messages, videos, photos, and so on, without any central server. Keet is the first messaging application that can scale to 8 billion people and eventually to machines, at no cost because it doesn't have any data centers. It cannot be blocked. It works everywhere, even under the strictest, most stringent types of internet control. We built this for people. We built this for fathers who want a reliable and secure way to talk to their children, but also for people working and living in potentially dystopian or authoritarian regimes. This is truly the first unstoppable communication application. I want to say one thing. One of the main criticisms of the Keet application has always been: why not open source? I can assure you, I am committed to making it open source. We are working on all the documentation, we are working on the individual modules, so that once it's open source, everyone can take it and improve it, build on it, change it, and recreate it in a single, simple step. Here's an example. This is a huge amount of work the Tether team has done to open source it, and we are doing it from the bottom of our hearts because we believe that if people can't talk to each other, if people can't send messages to each other, if we block the ability to communicate peer-to-peer—just like society was born from peer-to-peer communication. We meet on the street, we meet in the city square, we talk to each other without any intermediaries. For the past 50 years, intermediaries have taken over the financial world and the communications world. That's why society is in its darkest hour, because instead of using technology to create a more open society, we have hijacked technology, making society tightly controlled by a few companies. So Keet, and Keet's open source, will be one of the most important moments for us, one of the greatest testaments to our dedication to this mission. Then there are financial instruments, powered by WDK, serving humans, machines, and the agents between them to integrate unstoppable Bitcoin payments. WDK is one of our most successful products; it's an open-source library that allows any developer, anyone, any computer, and any AI agent to have a self-custodied wallet. We believe that in the future, billions of people, billions of machines, and trillions of AI agents will need a self-custodied wallet. People need control over their wealth. People need to be able to transact with anyone they want. So we wanted to create something that everyone could use, supporting any asset, but most importantly, supporting Bitcoin. From a purely physics perspective—technology is physics—from a purely technophysics perspective, we know the best way to scale future payment demands is when trillions of payments will be needed every day as AI agents become ubiquitous, and current financial transmission layers will be unable to handle it. Something like the Lightning Network is the right direction because it's Bitcoin's peer-to-peer transaction layer. I'm excited to see WDK supporting this from the very beginning. We want to ensure that your smart refrigerator, smart car, and smartphone can be transacted with Bitcoin, while protecting your Bitcoin and ensuring it always belongs to you. At the very top is decentralized artificial intelligence. Because if it's not your AI, it's not your intelligence. The final part is QVAC. We just released the QVAC SDK, an open-source software development kit that allows anyone to build AI tools that run locally on your smartphone, your device, and your laptop, thus guaranteeing your privacy. It supports all the best open-source large language models. It scales from the smallest GPU you own to your laptop, smartphone, all the way to mainframes. It embeds the HolePunch protocol, it embeds WDK, it embeds everything we've seen before. Because we drew inspiration from "If it's not your key, it's not your coin"—and we all know what that means for Bitcoin—we replicated the same concept, we understood: if it's not your AI, it's not your intelligence. We're driven—these days, these weeks, these years—we've been talking about AI. We know it's one of the most fundamentally transformative technologies for the future of society and humanity. But let me reiterate, if we don't build AI tools that cover the needs of that segment of the population—that half of humanity without access to basic financial services—if we pretend that those without access to basic financial services are also those who can't afford OpenAI or Anthropic subscriptions, these people cannot be left behind. They need tools that can work on their small smartphones, tools that can work in the most remote and smallest villages in Africa, Central South America, or Southeast Asia. We want Tether to build a coherent story, from telecommunications to messaging, to wallets and self-custodial wallets, to AI, creating a complete, unique, and fully open-source stack that empowers people, not companies, not enterprises, but simply people. Because ultimately, it comes back to science fiction: people will rebuild the universe, people will rebuild society, or rather, they will save society on Earth. People will make society on Earth more stable, without looking at light-years away from our solar system. So I suggest and recommend that each of you take a look at what we are building. Contribute, and again, it's all completely open source, on the Tether website. We will soon release funding so everyone can contribute to this technology and build upon it. We will be hosting a hackathon, and we will do everything we can to help people own what we build, be proud of their contributions, and ensure this technology remains resilient even in the face of God's wrath. Thank you very much. Have a great conference.

data.title: Strategy CEO: Digital lending is a killer app that will drive the adoption of Bitcoin.
data.description:
Compiled by: Jinse Finance

Michael Saylor, founder and CEO of Strategy, delivered a speech at the 2026 Bitcoin Conference in Las Vegas. In his speech, he positioned Strategy's perpetual preferred stock, STRC, as a rapidly growing Bitcoin-backed credit product, aiming to attract billions of dollars in retail investors and expand the scale of digital credit, making it a major driver of Bitcoin adoption and price growth.

Michael Saylor shared the latest developments in digital credit, digital income, digital currencies, and the digital transformation of capital markets in his speech.

He pointed out that the digital credit industry was officially born in the past 12 months. Its essence is an engineered ideal credit system, built by integrating existing technologies and tools such as listed companies, Bitcoin, perpetual preferred stock, monthly variable dividends, and capital return tax processing, combined with mechanisms such as shelf registration and ATM programs. It is a killer application of digital capital, consistent with the construction logic of Bitcoin (ideal digital capital). Michael Saylor highlighted Strategy's core tool, STRC, which combines the double-digit returns and tax efficiency favored by equity investors with the low volatility and capital preservation advantages valued by credit investors. It addresses the pain points of poor liquidity, lack of transparency, and high costs in private lending, offering high liquidity, transparency, homogeneity, scalability, and no fees. It has already achieved rapid growth, reaching $8.5 billion in size within eight months, becoming the world's largest and most liquid preferred stock, with daily liquidity approaching $400 million, volatility reduced to 2.9%, and a Sharpe ratio as high as 2.7, far exceeding other credit instruments and mainstream assets. Michael Saylor further elaborated on the operational logic of digital lending: Based on digital capital such as Bitcoin (which has risen 38% annually over the past 5 years), approximately 11% of the initial returns are extracted as a stable cash flow for lending investors, while excess returns belong to equity investors. Risk is mitigated and volatility suppressed through over-collateralization, while tax deferral is achieved through capital returns and dividends, creating a powerful compounding effect. This provides a high-quality option for various groups, including risk-averse investors, retail investors, corporate finance departments, and institutional investors. Currently, 3 million households have benefited, with 80% of holders being retail investors. Furthermore, Michael Saylor introduced the development prospects of digital income and digital currencies, pointing out that both are built on digital lending and can be presented in various forms such as tokenization, funds, and bank accounts. They can flexibly adjust volatility, yield, and liquidity, and many companies have already entered this market, resulting in explosive growth. **Strategy's vision is to provide high-yield savings accounts to billions of households and businesses through digital lending, **drive the expansion of the Bitcoin network, and facilitate the digital transformation of capital markets**, ultimately achieving the goal of "fixing money, fixing the world." This involves enabling Bitcoin to support various stablecoins, crypto tokens, and traditional credit instruments, providing global investors with new alternatives to traditional low-yield, high-risk assets.** The following is the full text of Michael Saylor's speech, compiled by Jinse Finance (with Deepseek assistance). It's a pleasure to be with you all today. My presentation will focus on the latest developments in digital lending, digital income, digital currencies, and the digital transformation of capital markets. I believe the past 12 months have been extraordinary, and one important reason for this is the emergence of the digital lending industry within these 12 months. I'd like to start by talking about digital lending and why it has become possible. **Digital lending is a killer application of digital capital. Bitcoin represents ideal capital. It represents engineered capital. It represents digital capital. As you know, it's a combination of technologies: proof-of-work, public-key cryptography, peer-to-peer networks, and distributed timestamps. By combining these components that have been around for some time, Satoshi Nakamoto was able to create an ideal capital asset—a non-sovereign store of value, a credential asset free from counterparty risk.** Digital lending works similarly. It's engineered lending. It's ideal lending. It's digital lending. How do we build it? We start with some off-the-shelf technologies. Publicly traded companies, which have been around for 100 years. Capital assets on balance sheets, for which we chose Bitcoin. Perpetual preferred stock, which has been around for centuries. We used monthly variable dividends, a method that has always worked but no one had ever thought of putting it into a lending instrument like the STRC. We used a standard tax treatment called "capital return," which has been around for over a hundred years. Then we combine all of this with shelf registration, ATM programs. These things have been around for a long time. It's just that no one ever thought of combining them with credit instruments. By combining all of these things, we are able to create digital credit. What is credit? What is capital? The world is built on capital. The world depends on credit to function. Capital is for those who want to make long-term investments and don't need cash flow; they will bear all currency risk, all duration risk, all volatility. They will bear all uncertainty over the long term because they have a lower time preference. There is a group of people and a segment of investors who want this kind of capital investment. But credit is for those with a much shorter time preference. They don't want risk, they don't want volatility; they want stable cash flow. They don't want to bear volatility and anxiety. They have immediate bills to pay; they want cash flow right now. So, our company, Strategy, transforms capital into credit. We transform commodities like BTC into currencies like the US dollar or the euro. We dollarize it. We take on 1:1 risk and then strip away the risk through over-collateralization. If you collateralize something at a 5x ratio, it means your capital asset can fall by 80%, and you still have full collateral. Capital investors lose 80% of their collateral, but credit investors are still protected. So you're stripping away risk. When you do this, you suppress volatility. Bitcoin's volatility has always been 40. When we strip away risk by anchoring to a standard value, we also strip away that portion of volatility. Then we extract a yield, a cash flow, from it while compressing duration. You don't wait ten years for capital gains; you get them in a month. The world is built on capital. The world operates on credit. We've designed many credit instruments, but after many trials, we finally found the STRC. The STRC is designed to provide the benefits that equity investors favor, such as double-digit returns and tax efficiency, while also offering the benefits that credit investors favor, such as low volatility and capital preservation. If you can combine all of these, you have the best of both the credit and equity worlds in one instrument. Digital lending is attractive to those who might prefer private lending. Private lending is about pursuing returns. People who don't like money markets, investment-grade bonds, or junk bonds will buy private lending. There's currently $3.5 trillion or more in private lending. But private lending is illiquid, opaque, and highly heterogeneous. It's a portfolio of thousands of private loans, discrete, limited to accredited investors, and expensive. Just in the last month, we've seen problems in the private lending market. They've experienced a wave of redemptions and are unable to meet redemption demands. Yet, there's still $3.5 trillion wanting to invest in this type of instrument. Digital lending, on the other hand, is liquid, transparent, homogeneous, scalable, accessible to everyone, and free. So, if you look at this, you'll see that even if digital lending only converts 10% of private lending, that's $350 billion in today's dollars. Digital lending aims to attract many different investors. It targets retail investors, digital-native or crypto investors, attracts hedge funds and hybrid investors, institutional credit investors, and corporate Treasurers and CFOs looking to hold some high-performing monetary asset on their balance sheets. Many are surprised that we use preferred stock to do this. But it turns out that digital credit is simply a reintroduction of preferred capital after 100 years. If you go back to the 19th century and think about the last major capital development activity—railroads—most railroads and many early industries during the Industrial Revolution were financed by preferred stock. Preferred stock once accounted for 20% to 40% of a company's capital structure. By the 20th century, preferred stock had fallen out of favor and was almost forgotten. Now, in the 21st century, we've reintroduced the concept of preferred credit, or preferred stock, into the capital markets. So, how did you create it? People always wonder, how did you create a credit instrument with an 11% yield? You start with the performance of asset classes. If you look at Bitcoin's performance over the past 5 years, it has risen 38% annually, which is much better than gold or the S&P 500. Real estate rises 6% annually. The money market rises 3% annually. You can't create a credit instrument with a dividend payout ratio higher than the return on the capital invested in its credit. So, as you can see, the highest theoretical yield that gold-backed credit can pay is 16%. Real estate credit can theoretically pay a maximum of 6%. On the other hand, for Bitcoin, the theoretical yield is 38%. So, if you're thinking about the theory of asset-backed credit or digital credit, what you're doing is taking your expected capital return on the capital asset and then paying a portion of that to credit investors. If you expect a 30% return on Bitcoin, you can pay 11% to credit investors. The excess return—here, the 19% yield difference between 30% and 11%—goes to equity. So common equity investors get the actual return or yield increase. Credit investors get that first 11% return, along with risk management and principal protection. Theoretically, you could do what we did with gold, real estate, or the S&P 500, but you couldn't pay that much in dividends because those asset classes don't perform that well.

This is another way of looking at it. Bitcoin is suitable for those who want to hold it for ten years.

... It's like a rollercoaster, with a 30% annual return and 30% to 40% volatility. You'll experience rising years and falling years. You won't have any cash flow for ten years. Digital lending simply strips away the first 11% of the returns and returns it to lending investors, giving you a very comfortable ride. If you look at that chart, we're just processing a financial signal. Where does the excess volatility go? Where does the excess return go? It goes to equity. So we create digital lending from digital capital, and digital lending creates digital equity. It turns out that all three assets are created using digital intelligence. Without digital intelligence, we cannot create digital lending. So if you're looking for the killer application of artificial intelligence, it's using digital intelligence to process digital assets or digital capital to create digital equity and digital lending. You can see how it works here. In fact, we've already done this with our own securities. Now, if you think about what this means for investors: If your investment horizon is less than four years, and you need the money within a month, a quarter, or a year, you might want to hold credit because you don't want any volatility in your principal. If you don't need the money for ten years, and you don't want counterparty risk, and you want complete sovereignty, you should buy a commodity like Bitcoin. On the other hand, if you have a long-term investment vision but want to bet on the future of digital capital and digital credit in a scaled-up manner, you buy equity, i.e., digital equity instruments. Many people have different opinions; you have to consider Bitcoin as one pole and credit as the other. Those who want absolutely no counterparty risk and complete sovereignty want commodities. But in your life, you are always buying things from companies, and you rely on the performance of those companies. When you board a plane, you trust the pilot to land safely. When you buy an iPhone, you trust Apple not to shut down your iPhone. When you go to the dentist, you trust the dentist not to stop halfway through. The world is full of such examples. When you watch a Netflix series, you trust Netflix to let you finish the show. When you buy electricity, you trust the power company to keep supplying power. In fact, I'd say there are a lot of people who want unlimited free electricity. They don't want to install a nuclear reactor in their backyard. So, what's happening here is that we've created a crypto reactor and used it to create credit to serve the people who don't want to do the work themselves. They don't want to wait ten years to actually cash out their investment. They want to cash out their investment every month for 120 months straight. Who would do that? Retirees, three-year-olds, twelve-year-olds, conservative institutions, companies with ongoing monthly bills to pay—companies that go bankrupt if they don't pay salaries for two months. People who go to jail if they miss a bill, right? There are a lot of institutions, individuals, and investors who can't afford long-term capital investments. For them, they need credit. An unexpected consequence we've discovered in this process is that if you fund the dividends of a credit instrument by monetizing unrealized capital gains, you create a capital-returning dividend that is tax-deferred. Capital return dividends have been around for over a century, but we've proven to be the company that figured out how to scale it by combining credit instruments with digital capital. So how has it performed? Here are the recent figures. **STRC grew from zero to $8 billion in about nine months. Daily liquidity is now close to $400 million. Its volatility has dropped to 2.9%. The Sharpe ratio is a whopping 2.7. Overcollateralization is 4x. Here's another chart. This is super-fast growth. How do you know a product works? It's growing at 350% per year. That's how fast it's growing. It's like 100% growth per month. I don't know how long this super-fast growth will last, but right now it's the fastest-growing credit instrument in the world, maybe the fastest in a decade or a century. Why is it growing so fast? Because it's an ideal form of credit. Because it's designed to provide everything credit investors want—if you're looking at it from an investor's perspective, not an issuer's, right? Most credit investors, or most credit issuers, want to create instruments that are good for themselves and bad for investors.** We start with a blank slate, using digital capital, and then leveraging digital intelligence to design credit instruments that benefit investors. That's the ideal credit instrument. How do we know? This instrument grew into the world's largest preferred stock in eight months. Now it's worth 8.5 billion. I'll show you the 10 largest preferred stocks. If you look at this chart, you'll see that STRC is a simple, easy-to-remember name. The other stock codes are things like CPN or PW.PR.E. If you search for these, you won't even find half of them. You can barely find them on Google. If you can't find them, how do you buy or sell them? They're actually institutional products, never meant for you to buy. Some of them are labeled OTC, and buying them is even illegal, right? They weren't built for public investors. They were built for the institutional world of 20th-century credit trading. And the result? Nobody trades them. STRC isn't just the largest preferred stock; it actually became the most liquid preferred stock globally in eight months. Let me repeat. Number one globally. It's the most liquid. It's less than 12 months old. Look at who it's competing with: Wells Fargo, Bank of America, Fannie Mae, Citigroup, JPMorgan Chase. Its liquidity is 25 times that of the second-place contender. It's less than a year old, right? Like a toddler Superman beating everyone to a pulp. And that's because it's powered by Bitcoin. Now, if you dig a little deeper, you'll find that its daily trading volume accounts for 4.5% of its assets under management. So it's not just more liquid and larger. It's actually a faster, more powerful currency, and an order of magnitude more powerful. So you can see its superior engineering. What else has high liquidity? Bitcoin. The reason digital credit is highly liquid is because digital capital is highly liquid, and digital equity is highly liquid. This isn't accidental. They are all interconnected. If you want to build the former, you must—if you want to have high-powered credit, you must build it on high-powered capital assets. What other characteristics do you want in credit? You want it to be stable. So you can see that during the cryptocurrency bear market, Bitcoin peaked at $125,000 on October 6th last year, a 38% drop. STRC, on the other hand, remained at 0%, exactly at par. So you can see that credit instruments are things you can manage to preserve the value of your principal. That requires an issuer. That's where corporations come in. Capital instruments have no issuer, no counterparty, no one managing them. If you're a long-term investor, I'll tell you every day, buy Bitcoin, not this credit. But the truth is, most people aren't long-term investors who can withstand that kind of volatility. They just want to put their money in a bank account, earn 10% or 11% returns, and then forget about it and let others worry about it. STRC was built for them. You can see its stabilization process. This is what it's been like over the past eight months or so. It started off a bit bumpy, then gradually entered the target range. In January, it traded within the target range 90% of the time. February was a very difficult month; it spent 80% of the time within the range. Then in March and April, it stabilized. It was 100% within the target trading range in March and April. So you can see it's now in the target range. Of course, liquidity is everything. If I told you it was a great product, but you could only buy and sell $100,000 a day, that wouldn't make sense. You wouldn't let someone invest a billion dollars in something that takes ten years to exit. So you can see here that liquidity has grown eightfold in five months. That's super-fast growth, right? Record-breaking. Never before has liquidity for a credit instrument grown this way. It's going viral.

This chart shows the relationship between preferred stock returns and daily liquidity. You can see STRC stands out. It's the supernova of the credit world, and everything else is on the side wall—illiterate, mediocre stuff. The result is that the flow of this thing is exploding. This is the demand for STRC. In January, there was about $500 million in business demand per month, which is equivalent to $6 billion annually. In February, we were hit hard.

... That was a very tough month; Bitcoin pulled back significantly, and demand plummeted to $80 million. In March, demand jumped to $1.5 billion, turning into a product worth $18 billion annually. Imagine going from zero to $18 billion annually in the first year. Then in April, it reached $3.5 billion. $3.5 billion multiplied by 12, and suddenly, the annualized run rate went from zero to $38 billion annually. It's less than a year old. So it's clear we're currently in a period of hyper-growth. May will be interesting, June will be interesting, July… You can Google how many products in the world have gone from zero to $20 billion annually in their first year. Not many. It's incredibly difficult.

If you go back to the innovation of shelf registration, you'll find that before we started selling digital credit, the largest shelf registration for any credit instrument in history was $500 million.

... Then Strategy created a $2 billion register for Strike, a $2 billion register for Strife, a $4 billion register for Stride, and a $21 billion register for STRC. So you can see that this innovation, this idea of ​​registering credit instruments on a shelf, has never been substantially used in any capital market in the world. The fact is, the U.S. is the leader in shelf registration. So when I say in the world, I mean Japan didn't do it, Europe didn't do it. The U.S. is the leader. And in the U.S., what we just did is 40 times larger than the second-largest project anyone has ever tried. Again, we're less than a year old.

Why? Why is the demand so high? The yield on private credit is 8.5%. You can describe the entire credit market as "risk without return." Buying investment-grade bonds or corporate bonds, you get 80 basis points above the risk-free rate. Junk bonds, 200 basis points above. Private credit, 300 or 400 basis points above, and it's illiquid and all taxed. ...>

Then we launched a product that pays 11.5%, is tax-deferred, highly liquid, and transparent. If you're a taxpayer in Miami Beach, that's equivalent to a bank account paying you 18% interest. What you're seeing now is that we've created the short end of the yield curve, like a one-month Bitcoin bond, the risk-free rate in the crypto ecosystem. So, what's the free-market rate of capital, or the free-market cost of capital? It's 11.5% now. You can see what the risk-free rate is for every other currency: 3, 2, 1, 0. Now, if you're an investor, you can start imagining a world where I borrow euros at 2% and then buy STRC at 11%, keeping the difference; or borrow yen at 70 basis points and invest in 11% STRC, keeping the difference. Right? There's a huge arbitrage opportunity here. What if you live in New York City? STRC is like a bank, paying you 24% interest in New York City. What if you live in San Francisco? The tax-equivalent yield is 23.1%. Your money market pays you 3.6%. Bank deposits pay almost nothing. Why wouldn't you buy? Volatility. The volatility is too high. You can see that by the end of April, we had reduced volatility from 13 to 2.3. Our goal is to bring it below 1. We reached a range of around 1 a few weeks ago. The only instrument in the entire credit industry with a volatility of 1 is the money market. Now, it's worth discussing some financial theory. The Sharpe ratio is defined as the return on an instrument minus the risk-free rate, divided by volatility. It tells you how much return you get for the volatility or risk you take. What does a risk-adjusted return mean when the Sharpe ratio is above 1? It means you get a return that exceeds the volatility you take. You can see that the Sharpe ratio for STRC is 2.7. The best credit instrument has a Sharpe ratio of 0.5, right? That's 5 times better than the second-best credit instrument. 10 times better than most credit instruments. What about the money market? The money market has a negative Sharpe ratio. Money market issuers charge you so much, 20 or 30 basis points, that there's essentially no return. This is “risk without return,” a negative Sharpe ratio. Compare digital credit to stocks. The best stock in the world is Nvidia. Nvidia currently has a Sharpe ratio of 1.89, which is positive. None of the other companies in the Big Seven tech giants can do that. None of them can return the risk you take. Amazon’s volatility is 5 times its return. STRC outperforms all of them. And it’s a credit instrument. Imagine doing this with credit. Now look at assets. The S&P 500’s return is lower than its volatility, with a Sharpe ratio of less than 1. Bitcoin, currently, has a return lower than its volatility. The Nasdaq is the same. Gold is 0.4. Real estate, real estate is terrible, right? High volatility, low return, you know, 0.17%, a very weak 17 basis points. So you see, STRC’s Sharpe ratio is higher than all of these instruments. In fact, it’s a bit like monetary fuel, which means it’s competing with $300 trillion in credit, $100 trillion in equity, and real estate. Digital lending will erode and replace real estate capital, equity capital markets, credit capital markets, and money capital markets. You can see on the screen that most of them are just sitting there, with very flat yields, various risks and transparency issues, and some are illiquid. None of them can compare to digital lending. You can see that in this chart, right?

If you walk down the street and ask a hundred people, "Do you want a 30-year bond? Do you want something you have to hold for ten years to know if you'll get rich? Or do you want a bank account that pays you 10% interest?" The answer is that everyone wants a bank account that pays them 10% interest. Some professionals might feel they could do better with a portion of their funds. But every company, every individual, every institution has a lot of money they want to keep in the bank, preserve their principal, and expect three or four times the return of money market rates.

... An unexpected consequence of digital lending is that you can buy digital credit and receive dividends tax-free and deferred, you can reinvest tax-free, and you can compound your wealth on credit instruments in a tax-deferred manner—something that is typically impossible. You can't do this with bonds, you can't do this with preferred stock. That's normal eligible dividend distribution. So here's a powerful compounding effect. As you compound those dividends, you're lowering the cost basis of the instrument. You collect dividends until the cost basis of the instrument is zero. If you then pass the instrument on to your heirs, if your daughter or son inherits the instrument, they get an "increasing base." So you get $100 in tax-free dividends, and they get $100 in tax-free dividends. Over 20 or 30 years, you can collect $200 in tax-free dividends on a $100 investment. What does this mean? Look at this. You have $100, invest it in short-term Treasury bonds for 21 years, you pay taxes, and then reinvest the dividends. Twenty years later, you have $158, and you have $368 in after-tax cash flow. If you did the same thing with digital credit, after the same amount of time, your money wouldn't be 10 times, but 6 times. You would have $965, earning $107 annually, compared to an initial investment of only $100. So here's a very powerful intergenerational wealth transfer opportunity for risk-averse investors, credit investors, and those who never want to suffer significant drawdowns but want to compound their wealth in a very tax-efficient way. Now, let's talk about adoption. Who's buying this stuff? **80% of STRC is held by retail accounts. This is a retail explosion.** A few months ago, we counted 120,000 independent retail accounts. It's also being adopted on a large scale by corporate treasury departments, by institutional investors and credit indices, by crypto natives, and by a group of financial innovators. Our estimate is—first, there are many ways to buy it. You can buy it on E*Trade, Robinhood, Fidelity, or Charles Schwab. So all standard retail channels support it. You can buy it in 10 seconds. So it's easy. It spreads incredibly fast and is a very successful retail product. We estimate that three million households are currently benefiting from STRC. So, what is our vision? Our vision is to power millions, then tens of millions, then hundreds of millions of households with a high-yield savings account. It's a straightforward thing. Everyone wants more money. Everyone wants banks to pay them three times what they currently receive. There's no debate about that. Create a digital yield account or a cryptocurrency account. A billion people want this. We've had a good start: 3 million in 8 months. But we won't stop there. We'll also target businesses. If you're a company, you probably have most of your money in short-term Treasury bonds. Short-term Treasury bonds give you 3.5% before tax and 2% after tax. STRC is very attractive to these companies because it offers a tax-equivalent yield that's five times higher. As a corporate Treasurer, wouldn't you want 16% instead of 3.6%? So, obviously, this is becoming very interesting for many people. If you allocate one-third of your corporate funds to STRC, your cash flow doubles. If you can actually put all your money into STRC, you can generate nearly four times the cash flow. Imagine getting four times the cash flow from the assets you have to hold on your balance sheet. Every company has to hold those assets. They need to pay salaries, pay taxes, and have one, two, or three years of working capital. But they don't have to put their money in low-performing money markets if they have better options. So who does? Energy companies, crypto companies, Bitcoin companies. This is starting to spread very quickly. I'm talking about institutional investors. BlackRock and VanEck run two well-known, large credit funds. STRC is their third-largest holding, representing 2% to 6% of their entire credit index. So when money flows into these credit indices, that money flows into STRC, and then into Bitcoin. Therefore, I increasingly believe we will see indices move towards credit.

There are also a lot of ETFs. **21Shares embedded STRC into an ETF, which launched in Europe a few weeks ago. Strive is creating a digital income fund and will bring it to the US market. Several other interesting public funds are in the pipeline and will be listed in the coming months.** This might allow me to transition nicely into talking about currencies and yields. The opportunity lies in the fact that there are thousands of companies that can create their own digital currency instruments or digital income instruments, all powered by digital credit, which in turn is powered by digital capital. Look at what we've done here. We've broken down digital capital—35x? 39% annual return?—into equity and credit. Credit is 3x? 11% yield. Equity is 72x? 58% annual return. So you can see what happens when you break down a commodity into credit and equity instruments. Credit is the second layer, and we consider the third layer to be currencies and yields. There are many interesting third-layer applications that your company, any bank, any cryptocurrency exchange, any investment manager, or even an individual can implement.** We define digital currencies as having 0% volatility and daily liquidity. It's high-powered money, right? I can withdraw my money every day. I receive streaming dividends. The digital yield might be non-zero, and there might not be daily liquidity, but it's built on digital credit. You can imagine you can tokenize STRC, put it in a private fund, in a mutual fund, an ETF, put it in a bank account, and you can deploy it through any platform—through Binance, through Coinbase, through Cash App. You can deploy it through Commonwealth Bank of Australia, Deutsche Bank, JPMorgan Chase, or Morgan Stanley. You can list it on Nasdaq, Euronext, or the Nice Stock Exchange. You can adjust volatility up and down, you can adjust yield up and down. You want to adjust the yield to 30%, you can do it. You can lower the yield, and then you can modify or program the liquidity, from continuous to daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, yearly. We've already seen people doing all these things, right? So digital currency, it can exist as a coin, it can exist as a fund, it can exist as an account. When you start thinking this way, you realize that if you reduce it, you create a currency with 0% volatility and a 7-12% yield. Like a perfect stablecoin, a Bitcoin-backed stablecoin that pays 7.5% interest. There have just been some announcements about this. Companies are going to do this in the crypto ecosystem. Then you can see, why wouldn't you leverage it three times? You might collect $33 or $35 in dividends, pay $8 in interest, and keep $25 on a $100 investment. So you get a 25% yield. You just need to cycle it three times. Digital yields can do this too. Here are some examples of companies doing this: Apex is doing it, Saturn is doing it, Hermetica is doing it.
