There's a huge demand in the crypto economy for Bitcoin-backed yields. Some companies are leveraging this to generate yields on Bitcoin, and many more want to create stablecoin-backed yields. How do I get yields from my stablecoin? This is also a very straightforward thing, and Apex and Saturn are doing it. Of course, you can also innovate with mutual funds and private equity, and you're seeing tokenization happening right now. How fast? This chart is outdated; it went from zero to 200 million in the last four weeks and is now about to break 300 million. So, it's gone from zero to hundreds of millions of dollars. I think our assets under management could break a billion dollars in the next four to eight weeks. This is an explosive industry downstream of STRC. We're committed to helping everyone who builds an application on top of STRC, and we want to make it more frequent, more liquid, and less volatile. One way we think we can do this is by doubling the frequency of dividends, from once a month to once every two weeks. Your employer pays you every two weeks. So why shouldn't your assets pay you every two weeks? Right? Why wouldn't you want that? So, the result is this: instead of 12 periods of dividends and drawdowns, it becomes 24 periods. 24 periods, each with half the intensity and half the dividend, means that theoretically, we should be able to make this thing oscillate within a narrower range, which will reduce volatility. We hope to reduce volatility and increase liquidity. That's what we expect. In the physical world, when you double the frequency of something, that's called raising it an octave. A note an octave higher has its frequency doubled. It's a signal with higher energy and higher fidelity. That's what we're doing with STRC. I just want to say a few things: it will be the only one out of 921 preferred shares to pay dividends every two weeks. It will be the only one out of 24,000 U.S. stocks to pay dividends monthly. It will be the only one to pay dividends every two weeks. So we're innovating in frequency, engineering, and design. But ultimately, the great innovation will be for the people who build funds, coins, and tokens on top of it, because they can achieve the frequency of hourly streaming payments. They can achieve the frequency of once an hour, 365 days a year. They can degrade it, leverage it, cycle it, convert it to yen, euros, dollars, or whatever you want. So we're very excited to provide a stable platform on which everyone else can build. This will be put to a vote, which will end in early June. If approved by shareholders, the first record date will be the end of June, and the first payment date will be July 15th. Voting has already begun. So if you are a STRC holder and want to express your opinion, you can vote on our website, or you can vote here. Speaking of which, I'll end with this idea: Digital credit is a killer application for Bitcoin. We expect to sell tens of billions of dollars in digital credit, until we sell hundreds of billions of dollars. If we sell hundreds of billions of dollars in digital credit, then we will continue to see if we can sell trillions of dollars. Every dollar that goes into digital credit will flow into digital capital. It will flow into the Bitcoin network. As it flows into the Bitcoin network, the price of Bitcoin should rise. We expect digital credit to drive the scale of the Bitcoin network. What is the ultimate goal? The ultimate goal is not complicated. To provide a billion people with a high-yield digital currency bank account with an annual return of 8% to 10%. To push Bitcoin to $10 million per coin, making Bitcoin a $200 trillion network, and so on, until it grows even higher. Providing everyone in the world with an alternative to 20th-century credit instruments, zero-yield bank accounts, mediocre junk bonds, private lending, contingent, precarious equity, or all challenging, difficult-to-manage, illiquid, immobile real estate investments. These things collectively represent the digital transformation of all capital markets. As we like to say in this industry and the Bitcoin community: Fix money, fix the world. Digital credit is the next killer app for fixing money. It will spread Bitcoin around the world. It will enable Bitcoin to support all stablecoins, all crypto tokens, and all kinds of traditional credit instruments. It will be a great day when the world's major banks and major investors lend some digital credit or offer Bitcoin-powered digital bank accounts. It is all of you who make this possible, and you inspire me every day. So, thank you. I am grateful to be on this journey with all of you. Every year, this community gathers to celebrate, debate, and build what's next. Every year, the stage grows bigger. Sound currencies take center stage. So, where will you go to celebrate the next chapter in Bitcoin's history?

data.title: Tiger Research: The DeFi frenzy is over; RWA delivers real value.
data.description:
Authors: Henry Kim, Ryan Yoon; Source: Tiger Research; Compiled by: Shaw, Jinse Finance

Key Takeaways

  • The USDC deposit rate for Aave V3 is currently 2.7%, lower than the 4.3% yield on the 10-year US Treasury bond. The short-term gains from DeFi driven by speculation are fading.

  • The market is not dead.

  • While overall yields are declining, Real-World Assets (RWAs) and stablecoins have grown into a multi-billion dollar sector, and the industry is moving towards a new direction. The failures of projects like Compound, Curve, and Olympus reveal a profound lesson: a model relying on token mutual support will collapse instantly once external incremental funds cease to flow in. Past DeFi was like a power strip without an external power source; RWAs are connecting this circuit to a real external value grid. The industry is maturing: using RWAs as a value anchor while gradually establishing collaborative governance and accountability mechanisms, as exemplified by industry initiatives like DeFi United.

1. Declining Returns, Market Growth

Decentralized Finance (DeFi) is no longer a high-yield product.

Since 2022, the yield spread between DeFi and government bond yields has narrowed to near zero, and at times even yield inversion has occurred.

**As of April 2026, the USDC deposit rate on Aave V3 was approximately 2.7%, lower than the US Federal Funds Rate (3.5%) and the US 10-year Treasury yield (4.3%).** In the past, users took on risk with a clear return logic. At that time, on-chain returns were far higher than bank deposits, with unparalleled advantages. However, the situation has now reversed. If we factor in various on-chain risks such as hacking attacks and stablecoin de-pegging, the actual return rate of DeFi is actually lower than that of traditional financial products, significantly weakening the motivation for ordinary retail investors to actively participate in DeFi. However, the entire industry is continuing to develop in a completely new direction. While native DeFi returns are declining, **Real-World Assets (RWA)** are deeply integrated with the stablecoin market into traditional finance, and their scale has climbed to hundreds of billions of dollars. The entry of institutional funds is the core factor driving this shift. However, institutions often overlook the development history and native community ecosystem of DeFi, blindly copying the rules and paradigms of traditional finance. Before the large-scale entry of institutions, DeFi was a market driven primarily by token incentives. Many protocols relied on incentive mechanisms to gain market recognition and reshaped the operating logic of the entire industry. This model still profoundly influences DeFi today. Aave, the leading protocol born in the DeFi summer, has now become the benchmark interest rate for the entire DeFi industry. For new institutional participants, a deep understanding of the core players who have survived the cycles and remain to this day is a necessary foundation before entering the market. This article will review the key protocols that shaped the core narrative of the industry throughout the complete development cycle of DeFi and summarize the lessons learned from them. 2. The History of DeFi Development: From Experimentation, Collapse to Reshaping DeFi was not initially built on token incentive promises. The starting point was quite simple: could we autonomously complete asset lending, exchange, and staking on the blockchain without intermediaries? In its early stages, the industry leaned more towards a financial experiment. The core value lay in the model itself: lending without bank involvement, asset exchange without centralized exchanges, and liquidity provided autonomously by any user holding collateralized assets. However, after 2020, the market landscape shifted rapidly, with token incentives becoming the core means of attracting funds. A massive number of protocols and innovative ideas emerged, but ultimately only a few projects survived the cycle. The industry learned lessons and continuously adjusted its development direction through repeated narrative shifts. Compound integrated its native token $COMP into its incentive system to attract liquidity on a large scale. However, when similar projects replicated this approach, new capital inflows dried up, and the structural fragility of its model was fully exposed. Curve transforms the governance voting mechanism into a battleground for distributing returns among various liquidity pools, turning the competition for returns into a struggle for control of the protocol. The market has thus realized that DeFi governance can also become a tool for power and incentive monopolies. OlympusDAO is the most extreme example. This project attempted to verify the feasibility of DeFi operating without external capital and controlling liquidity with extremely high annualized returns. However, the vast majority of its returns did not come from real cash flow but relied on issuing new tokens and new deposits. Once the inflow of funds slowed, the price of the governance token OHM collapsed, and market confidence in the protocol completely crumbled. These three projects collectively sound the alarm for the industry: if the core source of returns is the protocol's native token, this business model will ultimately be unsustainable. This past experience completely reshaped the understanding of DeFi among ordinary users, development teams, and institutional investors. It was precisely after this bubble burst that new tracks emerged: EigenLayer, Pendle, YBS, and RWA. 2.1. Compound: A Bubble Built on Token Distribution In June 2020, Compound began distributing governance tokens to users, with both depositors and borrowers receiving token rewards. At certain stages, COMP rewards even exceeded borrowing costs, giving rise to the peculiar phenomenon of making money through borrowing. This pioneered a completely new industry paradigm. With a massive influx of users, Ethereum on-chain transaction fees skyrocketed, with single transaction fees often reaching tens of dollars. Deposits and loans ceased to be simple financial operations, instead becoming tools for mining rewards, with capital chasing high returns rapidly flowing between various protocols. This period is known in the industry as the "DeFi Summer." Projects such as Uniswap, Aave, and Yearn Finance emerged one after another, and on-chain finance officially gained a foothold, developing into an independent sector. However, the model ultimately built by Compound essentially relied on token incentives to attract funds, which in turn drove up token prices, creating a positive cycle. The highly sensitive behavior of DeFi users today regarding yield, liquidity, and reward mechanisms gradually solidified during this stage.

2.2. Curve and veCRV: The Beginning of the Curve War

Curve was initially just a trading platform focused on stablecoin exchanges, but the advent of veCRV completely rewrote its underlying logic. The longer a user locks CRV, the more veCRV they receive; and veCRV represents weighted voting rights, which can determine the proportion of CRV rewards distributed among different liquidity pools.

... From this point on, the core of industry competition was no longer the highest or lowest yield, but the power to control the distribution of returns. Entities holding large amounts of veCRV could channel more token rewards to their own liquidity pools. Various protocols began hoarding veCRV, leading to fierce competition and the start of the Curve Wars. Initially, this mechanism was quite attractive to both retail investors and project teams: the longer retail investors locked their tokens, the higher their returns; project teams could use this to reduce the circulating supply of tokens and direct liquidity to target liquidity pools. As a result, similar lock-up governance models quickly spread within the ecosystem, such as Balancer's veBAL and Frax's veFXS. However, over time, governance power was no longer in the hands of ordinary users. Meta-protocols like Convex began collecting and locking CRV on behalf of users, using higher bonus returns as leverage to concentrate veCRV voting power. The Curve war escalated further, with the main battlefield shifting to Convex. veCRV ultimately confirmed a core conclusion: the power to control returns is far more attractive than the returns themselves. Users no longer directly hold governance power, but instead entrust it to more efficient intermediaries like Convex. Curve also made the market realize that DeFi governance power itself can become an interest-bearing asset, and that such power is highly susceptible to centralized monopoly.

2.3. OlympusDAO: A Golden Age Built on Game Theory

Even after the introduction of Curve's veToken mechanism, liquidity remains the biggest long-standing problem for DeFi. Liquidity introduced from outside will immediately withdraw once a higher incentive appears elsewhere; this type of capital is essentially profit-seeking speculative capital.

OlympusDAO, which emerged in the second half of 2021, once attracted much attention as a solution to this problem.

Its core design includes three main elements: Protocol-Owned Liquidity (POL), where the protocol directly holds its own liquidity; a (3,3) game theory model, which advocates that all users must choose to stake and lock their tokens to achieve the optimal global result; and an ultra-high annualized return rate exceeding 200,000% in the early stages of its launch. However, this model ultimately proved unsustainable. OHM's returns heavily relied on the issuance of new tokens rather than real business cash flow. Its bond mechanism spawned a large number of copycat and forked projects, and the price of OHM eventually plummeted by more than 90%. After this experience, the mindset of developers and users completely changed: before pursuing "how high the returns can be," people began to prioritize examining the real source of returns.

2.4. EigenLayer and Pendle: From Horizontal Yield Mining to Vertical Leverage

This crash completely changed the behavior of retail investors. The gameplay from 2020 to 2022 was simple and brutal: mine incentives first, then cash out. It was common for the same user to spread their funds across multiple protocols simultaneously. Mining during that period was horizontal arbitrage: capital flowed rapidly between different protocols, chasing higher annualized returns.

After 2022, this model became drastically inefficient. Token incentives proved unsustainable, and airdrop competition intensified. Simply diversifying deposits across multiple platforms resulted in diminishing marginal returns. Market sentiment shifted, with capital seeking multi-layered returns on a single asset: re-staking staked Ethereum (stETH), reinvesting Liquidity Tokens (LRT) into DeFi, and splitting ownership of yield to capture points and potential future returns. EigenLayer and Pendle became key players in this transformation. Since 2024, EigenLayer has introduced a restaking architecture that allows staked ETH to be combined with Liquidity Tokens (LST) to earn additional rewards. In just about six months, its total value locked (TVL) surged from less than $400 million to $18.8 billion, clearly demonstrating that capital is shifting on a large scale from simple deposits to the re-staking sector. Pendle, on the other hand, splits its interest-bearing assets into **Principal Certificates (PT)** and **Yield Certificates (YT)**. PT corresponds to a near-guaranteed principal interest; YT encompasses all interest, mining rewards, and points during its term. Yield certificates become worthless upon maturity, but during the holding period, points and returns can be maximized. Even without understanding the complex underlying mechanisms, buying YT has evolved into a mainstream mining strategy that leverages both time and capital. Industry strategies have thus been rewritten: from casting a wide net and diversifying across multiple protocols, to focusing on a single target and leveraging multiple layers of returns through compounding. 3. Restructuring the Profit Model: RWA and YBS In the past, projects heavily relied on token incentives to increase Total Value Locked (TVL). As TVL increased, the protocol seemed to expand, and the token price rose accordingly. However, the core drawback remained: external liquidity came and went quickly, making it difficult to retain. Today, TVL is still an important industry indicator, but the industry focus has shifted entirely to: transaction fee revenue, real-world asset backing, and compliance capabilities. The key variable behind this is the entry of institutional funds. Institutions will rigorously scrutinize the source of revenue and the true quality of the underlying collateral assets. New generation products are being iterated and upgraded to simultaneously adapt to the needs of retail investors and the compliance requirements of institutions.

3.1. Real-World Assets (RWA): Institutional Entrance

Since 2024, traditional financial institutions such as BlackRock, Franklin Templeton, and JPMorgan Chase have entered the on-chain market using Real-World Assets (RWA) as their entry point. Their operating model involves tokenizing off-chain physical assets such as US Treasury bonds, money market funds, private credit, gold, and real estate, and issuing and circulating them on the blockchain.

The on-chain RWA market has grown from several billion dollars in 2022 to tens of billions of dollars in April 2026. Tokenization of government bonds and private lending are the core drivers of this growth. Currently, leading institutional-grade products are represented by BlackRock's BUIDL and Franklin Templeton's BENJI. While the underlying asset types of these two products are similar, their operating models differ: BUIDL is strictly geared towards institutional investors, while BENJI has a minimum entry threshold of only $20 and is open to ordinary retail investors in the United States. In addition, asset management giants such as Apollo, Hamilton Lane, and KKR are collaborating with on-chain issuance platforms like Securitize to accelerate the tokenization process of private equity funds and private lending. For traditional institutions, the on-chain market is not a completely new or unknown territory, but rather a brand-new asset distribution channel. Therefore, various protocols serving institutional clients are improving their supporting systems: building compliant Know Your Customer (KYC) and Anti-Money Laundering (AML) mechanisms, custody infrastructure, adaptability to all legal jurisdictions, and a professional risk management framework. 3.2. Interest-Bearing Stablecoins (YBS): USD Assets with Built-in Yields

3.2. Interest-Bearing Stablecoins (YBS): USD Assets with Built-in Yields

Currently, the most noteworthy niche market is interest-bearing stablecoins (YBS). Interest-bearing stablecoins are stablecoin products that directly embed the yield mechanism into the token itself.Ondo USDY, Sky sUSDS, Ethena sUSDe, and BlackRock BUIDL and Franklin BENJI mentioned earlier all belong to this category.

Users only need to hold these assets to automatically accumulate returns generated by the underlying assets. These underlying assets include US Treasury bonds, funding rate yields, collateralized interest, and money market funds. The entire architecture is essentially equivalent to migrating traditional money market funds (MMFs) on-chain. According to StableWatch's cumulative yield output (YPO) data, Ethena sUSDe, Sky sUSDS, BlackRock BUIDL, and Sky sDAI rank among the top products in terms of cumulative interest payments across the market. While data may vary slightly depending on the statistical method, there is no doubt that interest-bearing stablecoins have long since moved beyond the niche experimental stage and matured into a sector capable of consistently distributing real interest. Even so, simply putting money market funds on-chain does not constitute a core differentiating advantage. The real barrier lies in composability. BlackRock's BUIDL accounts for 90% of Ethena's USDTb reserve assets, and USDTb can be used as collateral to access the Aave lending ecosystem. In other words, what was originally a basic financial product used as a real-world asset instrument has now transformed into a stable underlying component of on-chain finance. DeFi no longer relies on its limited internal "built-in battery" to barely operate, but has begun to access external real value energy. 4. Building the RWA Value Grid: Learning from Historical Failures Before this, DeFi was always doing one thing: a **layered, self-nested power strip loop, euphemistically called a growth flywheel**. Layer upon layer of leverage and derivatives were stacked, with all links closed and self-consistent. The fatal problem was that the energy never came from outside; the vast majority of the returns were created out of thin air by the protocol's self-developed token incentives. Compound lends using its native token, while Curve uses its own token to retain liquidity providers. On the surface, the various parties mutually supply each other and operate in a cyclical manner; however, the entire system shares a single, limited-capacity battery. Once the market experiences a shock, the underlying value collapses first, cascading upwards until the final derivative products cease functioning. This self-enclosed, self-backed model has a natural upper limit to its capacity. RWA, for the first time, connects this system to a real external value grid. Real economic cash flows such as bond interest, property rent, and trade receivables become the stable power supply running through on-chain finance. Interest rate pricing is no longer artificially manipulated by internal token incentives but is jointly determined by external market supply and demand, macroeconomic interest rates, and credit risk. As stable cash flow continues to circulate, various financial modules such as issuance, custody, collateralization, lending, and settlement can be connected to this grid layer by layer. Many complex financial products that were difficult to implement in traditional DeFi are now feasible solutions based on the RWA underlying architecture. The core issue for the industry is no longer endlessly adding power strips and stacking layers, but how to obtain a long-term and stable current of value. This is the core essence of on-chain RWA: putting assets with real underlying value on the chain, using their continuously generated cash flow as a foundation, and then adding various financial business modules. If old-style DeFi relied on token incentives as a temporary battery to borrow liquidity to prolong its life, then the current RWA track is relying on the intrinsic cash flow of the assets themselves to achieve long-term liquidity accumulation. The leading players in the track are now building this new financial power grid from their respective tracks: Theo is responsible for selecting underlying assets that can be put on the chain, deciding which real assets to connect, and acting as the energy source of the entire power grid. Plume builds the underlying infrastructure for asset issuance and circulation, laying power transmission lines and dispatch hubs to ensure smooth value flow. Morpho, using circulated physical assets as collateral, builds a lending and mortgage market, becoming the first financial terminal on this new power grid to truly consume and utilize value. No single institution can monopolize the entire power grid. This new financial loop, called On-Chain RWA, can only be considered a complete closed loop when the energy source, transmission network, and application terminal are interconnected. 4.1. Theo: A Case Study of User Base Strategy Restructuring Theo is a typical example: starting with the selection of underlying assets, it completely reshaped its customer base and completed a comprehensive transformation. Theo's flagship product was initially the Strategy Vault. However, with changes in the market landscape, the needs of retail investors and institutions began to diverge significantly. Theo proactively adapted to industry trends and comprehensively repositioned its target customer group. Theo's current core product is thBILL. This product consists of a portfolio of institutional-grade tokenized short-term U.S. Treasury bonds provided by compliant issuers. As the core underlying asset of the Theo ecosystem, it continuously generates stable returns. The project roadmap has added thGOLD (tokenized gold), and the interest-bearing stablecoin thUSD, issued against thGOLD, will also be launched soon. This transformation is not merely a product iteration; it also confirms that projects originating from retail investor incentive programs can completely restructure their underlying architecture to adapt to and align with the compliance and business needs of institutions. 4.2. Plume: Building the Underlying Ecosystem for RWA Implementation Plume is another typical example, deeply integrating asset circulation infrastructure with upper-level market demands. For institutions, simply putting assets on the blockchain is far from enough; a complete infrastructure covering issuance, compliance, distribution, and yield productization is also needed. Similarly, for on-chain users wanting to invest in institutional assets such as government bonds and funds, a comprehensive product system is required. Nest is a yield protocol built on the Plume underlying infrastructure. Users deposit stablecoins to easily access yields generated by institutional-grade real-world assets (RWA). Its various vault products, including nBASIS, nTBILL, and nWisdom, each rely on different physical assets as the underlying yield; vault tokens can be freely transferred and circulated within the DeFi ecosystem. WisdomTree has launched 14 tokenized funds on Plume, Apollo Global Management has implemented a $50 million credit strategy, and Invesco has migrated its $6.3 billion advanced lending strategy to the Plume chain. Nest has become a core entry point for ordinary users to access these institutional assets. In addition to its own ecosystem, Plume is also a comprehensive integrated infrastructure, building a standardized distribution channel between institutional assets and on-chain funding needs. 4.3. Morpho: Adding Complete Financial Functions to Institutional Assets Morpho serves as the third typical case study, illustrating how assets can be transformed into collateral, lending instruments, and sources of liquidity. For institutions, registering assets on the blockchain is merely the starting point; the real key lies in whether these assets can serve as collateral and thereby release liquidity. Lending terms and risk parameters must be clearly defined, and all business operations must be legally implemented within a custodian and compliance framework. A prime example is Apollo's ACRED product. Apollo not only deploys credit strategies on Plume but also allows ACRED to be used as collateral in Morpho, enabling holders to borrow stablecoins while maintaining their fund holdings. ACRED is a tokenized private credit fund built on the Apollo diversified credit securitization fund, with its on-chain issuance completed by Securitize. Only when institutional assets can serve as collateral, support lending, and release liquidity can they truly become usable underlying materials for on-chain finance. 5. The Dopamine Craze Fades, What Remains in the Industry? Looking back, the golden age of DeFi was more like a mirage built on token incentives and leverage. Despite some voices within the industry still citing the recent hacking incidents as a reason to be pessimistic about the recovery prospects of DeFi, the handling of the Kelp DAO rsETH incident and the establishment of the DeFi United alliance present a completely opposite industry trend. As of April 28, 2026, Aave and DeFi United have successfully raised over $300 million, far exceeding the $190 million lost in the hacking incident. This clearly demonstrates that the industry is gradually building trust infrastructure, and a more mature collective accountability mechanism is emerging. Looking back at the development of DeFi, it can be seen that in its early stages, the industry was in a state of complete disorder with no accountability. Users' sole objective is to quickly acquire high-yield tokens; project teams, seizing this opportunity, design various high-yield mechanisms, often withdrawing once their fundraising goals are achieved. The industry is now undergoing a complete shift: **institutional accountability is being proactively incorporated into system design.** A complete and mature financial system is yet to be formed, but what is certain is that the industry has reached a consensus: **facing common risks squarely, reasonably sharing losses, and clearly defining responsibility.** Many people are bearish on the market not only because of frequent security vulnerabilities, but also because of the complete disappearance of short-term high returns and the market's lack of new narratives and growth catalysts. The influence of the broad concept of "DeFi" is continuously weakening. The market has been segmented into more precise vertical tracks: lending, stablecoins, real-world assets (RWA), restaking, on-chain lending, etc. The concept name is no longer important. Early innovative experiments originating from DeFi are steadily maturing and evolving into a sustainable underlying architecture, enabling more assets to truly enter the real economy and generate real value.

data.title: Republican Thom Tillis Is Threatening To Destroy The GENIUS Act Unless Trump Destroys His Crypto Business
data.description:
Republican Senator Thom Tillis, one of the key architects of the GENIUS Act, is threatening to destroy what he has created if Trump doesn't immediately end his crypto businesses.

🇺🇸 LATEST: Senator Thom Tillis warns he will vote against the Senate crypto bill unless it includes ethics language restricting officials' use of digital assets. pic.twitter.com/dFrb7XB6GO

— Cointelegraph (@Cointelegraph) April 27, 2026

Tillis said he is willing to oppose the Senate’s version of the Crypto Clarity Act unless strict ethics provisions targeting White House involvement in digital assets are included.

"There has to be ethical language in the bill before it leaves the Senate, or I'll go from one of the people working on the negotiating it to voting against it."

Trump-Linked Crypto Interests Become Legislative Flashpoint

At the center of the dispute is the growing scale of crypto ventures tied to Donald Trump and his family. Projects such as World Liberty Financial and the USD1 stablecoin have intensified scrutiny in Washington, with the family's combined crypto venture valued exceeding $1 billion.

Tillis’s demand aligns, in part, with Democratic calls for guardrails. Senator Adam Schiff has framed the proposal as a broad ban on federal officials sponsoring, endorsing, or issuing digital assets, while Senator Ruben Gallego has made clear that no final bill will advance without bipartisan agreement on ethics provisions.

What distinguishes this moment is that the pressure is no longer coming solely from Democrats. A senior Republican now stands at the center of the standoff, transforming what had been a partisan concern into a structural threat to the bill itself.

Can the Bill Advance Without Tillis?

The legislative math in the Senate leaves little room for defection. The US Senate requires 60 votes to advance major legislation, making bipartisan support essential. Tillis’s opposition, combined with firm Democratic demands, creates a scenario where the bill risks stalling regardless of leadership priorities.

Complicating matters further, the Senate Banking Committee—where Tillis holds influence—does not have jurisdiction over ethics provisions. This procedural limitation means the required language must be negotiated outside the standard markup process, adding another layer of political complexity before the bill can reach the floor.

Even as negotiations continue, with figures such as Patrick Witt and Republican Senators Cynthia Lummis and Bernie Moreno expressing how the bill is finally moving forward after a long stalemate, whilst it remains very slow and fragile.

"We're making progress. We have been talking for a long time without making much progress, and now that other parts of the bill are starting to come together, we're narrowing our differences. Progress, though, is not resolution."

Morgan Stanley has launched the Stablecoin Reserves Portfolio (MSNXX), a new government money market fund that aligns with the GENIUS Act.https://t.co/QJyZh1tUfG

— Rare Network (@RareNetworkWeb3) April 26, 2026

The bill is already contending with separate disputes, including disagreements over stablecoin yield provisions and the proposed regulatory split between the Commodity Futures Trading Commission and the Securities and Exchange Commission.

Without a resolution that satisfies both Tillis and Democratic lawmakers, the Crypto Clarity Act risks becoming the latest example of regulatory gridlock delaying institutional clarity for the digital asset sector.

data.title: Why do digital yuan and smart agents present opportunities for each other?
data.description:
Author: Zhang Feng

As the legal digital currency issued by the People's Bank of China, the digital yuan is reshaping the payment system with its unique attributes such as controllable anonymity, programmability, and payment-as-settlement. Intelligent agents (AI agents), as digital intelligence systems with autonomous perception, decision-making, and execution capabilities, are evolving from simple algorithmic models into digital entities capable of independently completing tasks. When the two meet, a deep interrelationship is forming: the digital yuan endows intelligent agents with value flow capabilities, while intelligent agents open up unlimited application scenarios for the digital yuan.

I. Digital RMB as an Opportunity for Intelligent Agents

The digital RMB empowers intelligent agents with independent value exchange capabilities. Its programmable payment, zero marginal cost, controllable anonymity, and offline transactions enable intelligent agents to bypass human intervention and autonomously complete condition-triggered small-amount, cross-border, or instant settlements.

data.title: Bitwise: BTC's recent rise was driven by STRC; can the momentum continue?
data.description:
Author: Matt Hougan, Chief Investment Officer of Bitwise; Translated by Shaw Jinse Finance

The recent surge in Bitcoin has been largely driven by Strategy's continued buying. Can this buying momentum continue?

Bitcoin has risen approximately 20% from its February lows and is currently trading at around $76,000. All parties in the market are watching to see if this upward trend can continue.

The answer largely depends on Strategy.

Admittedly, this rebound is driven by multiple factors, including strong net inflows into Bitcoin ETFs (totaling $3.8 billion since March 1st) and buyback demand from long-term holders. However, Strategy is the single most crucial driver, having accumulated $7.2 billion worth of Bitcoin over the past eight weeks. Its purchases primarily come from funds raised through the issuance of its perpetual preferred stock product, STRC. This article will detail the STRC product mechanism and analyze why this large-scale purchase is likely to continue for some time. What is STRC?

STRC is a perpetual preferred stock issued by Strategy. Preferred stock is a hybrid financial instrument, possessing characteristics of both bonds and stocks.

Strategy's core objective in issuing STRC is to maintain a stable price of $100 per share while providing investors with a high dividend (currently an annualized yield of 11.5%).

The company will dynamically adjust the dividend yield to anchor the stock price: If the STRC price falls below $100, the dividend yield will be increased to attract new buyers; if the price is above $100, additional shares will be issued or the yield will be lowered to guide the price back to the target level. Since its listing, STRC has generally maintained its $100 pricing target, with occasional deviations but overall controllable. To stabilize the stock price, Strategy has repeatedly increased the dividend: the initial annualized yield was 9%, which has now been increased to 11.5%.

STRC stock price trend since its listing

Data source: Bloomberg. Time range: July 25, 2025 to April 27, 2026.

Why did Strategy issue STRC?

The core purpose of Strategy's issuance of STRC is to continuously increase its Bitcoin holdings. The vast majority of the funds raised through the issuance of STRC will be used to buy Bitcoin on the open market. How does Strategy pay its 11.5% dividend? This is also the most perplexing key point: Strategy's main method of paying dividends is to continuously raise funds from new investors. Isn't this a Ponzi scheme? Not at all. The reason is that the preferred stock is backed by real-value assets—namely, Strategy's massive Bitcoin holdings. Currently, Strategy holds $63 billion worth of Bitcoin, has $8 billion in liabilities, and $14 billion in preferred stock. In corporate liquidation priority, preferred stock takes precedence over common stock. This means that if the company enters liquidation: the $8 billion in debt will be repaid first, followed by the $14 billion in preferred stock, leaving approximately $41 billion in assets for common stockholders. Will Strategy eventually run out of funds? The outcome varies and depends on Bitcoin price performance. Based on current Bitcoin prices, the company's cash reserves are theoretically sufficient to pay dividends for 42 years. In other words, if Bitcoin prices stagnate until 2068, Strategy will be in serious crisis; however, if Bitcoin maintains a 20% annual increase, it could theoretically cover dividend payments indefinitely. (Additional note: Under certain conditions, Strategy may suspend dividend payments, but dividend liabilities will continue to accumulate with compound interest and will be treated as normal interest accrual.) What would happen if Strategy continued to issue more STRC, or if the price of Bitcoin fluctuated wildly? This is precisely the core of the risk. Strategy's ability to stably pay STRC dividends depends on two major variables: the Bitcoin market and the scale of issuance. All other things being equal, the larger the amount of STRC issued, the higher the dividend liabilities, and the higher the risk of default; however, at the same time, the book profits from rising Bitcoin prices will increase the balance sheet, offsetting the aforementioned risks. In short: **buying STRC is essentially betting that Strategy can maintain a balance**—that it won't issue STRC without restraint and ultimately trigger a debt crisis. Will Strategy continue to issue more STRC? I believe it's highly likely. Market rumors suggest that Strategy could have raised even more funds in the previous STRC fundraising round if it had wanted to. With current junk bond yields below 7% and private credit funds continuing to flow out, STRC's high annualized yield of 11.5%, backed by over $40 billion in Bitcoin assets as a safety buffer, is extremely attractive. Therefore, I believe Strategy will raise several billion more dollars through STRC. How much room is there specifically? Key indicator: Total liabilities (debt + preferred stock) as a percentage of Bitcoin holdings. Currently, this ratio is 33%: $21 billion in liabilities, corresponding to $63 billion in Bitcoin holdings. Once this ratio approaches 50%, investors will begin to collectively question the risk. Based on the current cryptocurrency price, the company still has room to issue $10 billion to $15 billion more in STRC; if Bitcoin rises further, the fundraising cap will be further increased. In conclusion, we can be absolutely certain that STRC will continue to be issued.
